O-01.name: Supply chain concentration & disruption

supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.source: erp_supply_chain.csv — TSMC critical-path supplier (18-week lead time)
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.description: Inventory cover weeks for the critical-path single-source supplier (TSMC, 18-week lead time). Amber threshold 8w = TSMC target_inventory_weeks from ERP. Breach threshold 4w = minimum safe operating level (~22% of TSMC lead time). Current 5.2w = amber: 2.8 weeks below target, 12.8 weeks below TSMC lead time — a TSMC disruption today results in production stop in 5.2 weeks with no substitution path.
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.threshold_rationale: TSMC 18-week lead time benchmark: amber at target inventory (8w), breach at 22% of lead time (4w)

O-01.agent_findings:
OPERATIONAL RISK ASSESSMENT — PERIOD ENDING 2026-05-28

O-01 SUPPLY CHAIN — ESCALATION TRIGGERED

Two of three KRIs are in breach. Single-source concentration stands at 62.6% of $12,610M total spend, against a breach threshold of 50%. This is not a marginal breach — it exceeds the threshold by 12.6 percentage points and represents $7,893M of spend exposed to single-source failure. The three single-source suppliers are TSMC (Semiconductors-CPU, $4,200M, Taiwan, 18-week lead time), Foxconn (Manufacturing-Assembly, $2,100M, China, 8-week lead time), and Quanta (ODM-Laptops, $1,600M, Taiwan, 6-week lead time).

Minimum inventory cover across the portfolio is 3.9 weeks, breaching the 4-week floor. The minimum cover belongs to Quanta, which is also the most financially distressed single-source supplier at health score 61 — below the distress flag threshold of 65. Quanta simultaneously satisfies both conditions of the single-source escalation trigger: financial_health below 65 and inventory below 4 weeks. This is escalated immediately.

The consequence profile is severe. TSMC carries an 18-week lead time against 5.2 weeks of cover. A TSMC disruption — whether from geopolitical event, natural disaster, or financial stress — creates a hard production stop approximately 5 weeks after event onset, with no credible alternative sourcing available inside the 18-week lead time window. The supply chain stress model quantifies revenue at risk at $9.1B at the 95th percentile VaR of $2,634M. Recovery is estimated at 5 months. The model further indicates that dual-sourcing remediation could recover $382M in savings and inventory buffering a further $305M, establishing a clear financial case for accelerated action.

Geographic concentration compounds these concerns: 53.5% of spend is Taiwan-domiciled (TSMC and Quanta both), meaning a single-geography shock removes the two most critical suppliers simultaneously. China at 21.8% adds Foxconn exposure.

Yangtze Memory [SUP008], a multi-source supplier, carries a financial health score of 55 — the lowest in the portfolio — and contributes to the two active distress flags (amber threshold: 1, breach threshold: 3). While not yet at breach on distress count, the trajectory of Yangtze Memory warrants proactive monitoring given its proximity to the breach threshold.

Trend: Deteriorating. Two KRIs in breach, no evidence of active dual-sourcing remediation programme in the data provided, and the demand shock probability sits at 24% with a 15% impact if triggered.

---

O-02 CYBER AND IT — ESCALATION TRIGGERED

Four KRIs are in breach; three are amber. The breach profile is wide and structurally concerning.

Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) is 11 days, against a breach threshold of 10 days. With 4 security incidents recorded month-to-date, threats are not hypothetical — they are active. An 11-day detection window means an adversary who gains access today will, on average, operate undetected for 11 days. Mean Time to Respond is 18 days, meaning the full detect-to-contain cycle spans nearly three weeks on a per-incident basis.

Patch compliance stands at 71%, against a breach threshold of 80%. This means approximately 29% of systems are running unpatched. Combined with 8 critical vulnerabilities open beyond 30 days (amber threshold 5, breach threshold 10 — this sits in amber territory at 8 but is approaching breach), the attack surface is materially exposed.

IT RTO is 6 hours — exactly at the breach threshold of 6 hours. There is no buffer. A major incident today would push recovery beyond the acceptable window.

The most structurally dangerous finding is supplier cyber resilience assessment at 0%. Not a single supplier has been assessed for cyber resilience, yet 4 active security incidents have occurred this month. This meets the escalation trigger condition directly. With TSMC, Foxconn, and Quanta all deeply integrated into production systems, an undetected compromise of their environments — invisible to the organisation because no assessment framework exists — could manifest as supply disruption, data exfiltration, or operational sabotage with no early warning.

MFA coverage at 87% and privileged access unreviewed for 45 days add to the control gap. 13% of the estate lacks MFA, and unreviewed privileged access for 45 days is a significant lateral movement and insider threat risk.

The O-02 + O-04 interconnection is critical here: IT-Operations attrition at 11.8% and 7 critical roles open beyond 60 days mean the team responsible for patching, incident response, and vulnerability management is understaffed. This directly explains the patch compliance breach and elevated MTTD — they are symptoms of a talent-constrained security function.

BCM implication: An RTO at exactly 6 hours means any unplanned major incident will breach operational recovery commitments with no margin. Revenue impact would materialise immediately for any outage extending beyond this window across order management systems (IT RTO cited as it_rto_oms).

Trend: Deteriorating. Four KRIs in breach, active incidents, 0% supplier assessment, and a talent pipeline issue suppressing control effectiveness.

---

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — MONITOR; NO SAFETY INCIDENTS

No confirmed safety incidents year-to-date. Zero regulatory product notices. Field failure rate of 0.019% is well within amber threshold of 0.05%. These are genuine positives and the zero-tolerance escalation trigger for safety incidents is not triggered.

However, three indicators warrant attention:

Recall readiness score of 72 is in amber territory, 8 points below the 80 amber threshold and only 2 points above the 70 breach threshold. The last recall simulation was conducted 8 months ago. The combination of a readiness score trending toward breach and an 8-month gap since testing means the organisation's ability to execute a recall — should one be required — is structurally untested and weakening. This is a latent risk: benign today, but the consequence of being unprepared when a quality event occurs is disproportionate in regulatory, reputational, and financial terms.

Supplier quality rejection rate is 0.68%, in amber (threshold: 0.5% amber, 1.0% breach). The specific rejection rates by supplier are visible in O-01 data: Quanta at 1.2% is the highest among single-source suppliers. Quanta's rejection rate is above the 1.0% breach threshold for supplier quality rejection individually. This is notable because Quanta is already flagged for financial distress and minimum inventory cover. A financially stressed supplier with rising rejection rates and thin inventory is a compounding signal, not three independent ones.

Warranty claims of 1,842 month-to-date are provided as a data point; no threshold is specified in the data for this metric, so no status determination is made beyond noting volume.

First pass yield of 97.2% and customer complaint rate of 0.12% indicate that internal production quality is holding. The quality risk is concentrated in the inbound supplier tier, particularly Quanta, rather than in own-manufacturing processes.

EBITDA implication: Warranty claims at 1,842 MTD represent a cost line. If Quanta's rejection rate or financial health deteriorates further, the proportion of defective ODM-Laptop units entering the fulfilment chain could increase, directly expanding warranty cost exposure.

Trend: Flat on safety; deteriorating on recall readiness (score approaching breach, simulation overdue) and supplier rejection (Quanta specifically above individual breach threshold).

---

O-04 TALENT — ESCALATION TRIGGERED

Three flight-risk employees are flagged simultaneously within R&D. This meets the escalation trigger of three or more flight-risk employees in the same critical function. This is escalated immediately.

Engineering attrition is 14.2% annualised — in amber (threshold: 12% amber, 18% breach). IT-Operations attrition is 11.8% — at amber boundary. Both functions are under sustained outflow pressure.

Critical open roles beyond 60 days stands at 7 across the organisation, breaching the threshold of 6. Average time to fill in Engineering is 68 days, meaning even when positions are identified and resourced, the fill cycle extends beyond the 60-day threshold by default. The organisation is structurally unable to close its own vacancy metric at current fill velocity.

Compensation gap versus market in Engineering is 8.2%. This is the clearest causal driver of attrition and flight risk. The counter-offer acceptance rate of 42% in Engineering confirms the dynamic: when departing engineers are counter-offered, fewer than half accept. Either the counter-offers are insufficient, or the departure motivation is non-financial (career, culture, management). Either interpretation is concerning.

SVP succession coverage at 72% is in amber (threshold: 80% amber, 70% breach) — one point above breach. If a single SVP-level departure occurs in a currently uncovered role, this metric breaches. With flight risk concentrated in R&D and an 8.2% compensation gap in the most senior technical functions, the proximity of succession coverage to breach is not theoretical.

Employee engagement at 67% and internal promotion rate at 18% are provided as context metrics. Low engagement and limited internal mobility are consistent with the attrition and flight risk profile and suggest the compensation gap is part of a broader retention challenge, not solely a pay issue.

BCM and revenue implication: R&D flight risk directly threatens the product development pipeline. Loss of 3 key R&D personnel in the same function creates knowledge concentration risk — particularly for capability that is not easily recruited externally in under 68 days. This also intersects with O-01: qualification of alternative suppliers for TSMC and Quanta requires R&D and engineering involvement. Talent loss in these functions will slow dual-sourcing remediation, prolonging the BCM vulnerability quantified in the supply chain stress model.

EBITDA implication: 8.2% compensation gap in Engineering. Closing the gap to market requires incremental compensation spend across the Engineering population. Failure to close it perpetuates attrition and associated recruitment, onboarding, and productivity-loss costs. The counter-offer acceptance rate of 42% suggests the organisation is already incurring counter-offer costs with limited retention return.

Trend: Deteriorating. Two escalation conditions present (flight-risk concentration in R&D; open roles in breach), compensation gap widening pressure, and succession coverage approaching breach.

O-02.name: Cyber attack & IT resilience

cyber_supply_response_gap_days.description: Days by which combined MTTD+MTTR cyber response time exceeds available inventory cover. Positive value = production halt guaranteed if cyber incident disrupts supplier comms. Amber: 0 (response just meets inventory); Breach: >0 (response exceeds inventory). Target: ≤ -2 days (2-day safety margin).

O-03.override_justification: Expert override applied 2026-06-07 (EM-08 panel finding). Arithmetic rating: low (l=2, i=5). Override to: high (l=3, i=5). Justification: Likelihood score of 2 is inconsistent with active breach conditions — warranty_claims_mtd at 1,842 (up from 1,612), recall_readiness at 72 (amber, 2 points from breach), recall simulation last run 8 months ago. Upstream supply quality (Quanta rejection rate 1.2%, Foxconn 0.8%) is a confirmed leading driver of downstream field defects. Per O-04 override precedent: forward-looking indicators and breach trajectory take precedence over lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off required next review cycle.

O-04.override_justification: Expert: High. Arithmetic: Low (+2 bands). Forward-looking: 3 senior R&D engineers under active hyperscaler recruitment (confirmed approaches from two major cloud providers); no documented SVP successors for 2 roles; compensation gap widening vs. hyperscaler benchmarks. Override reflects leading indicators not captured in lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off: 2026-06-07 (reissued EM-06 panel correction — prior text erroneously referenced 'senior traders'; corrected to R&D engineering population).

A TSMC disruption — whether from geopolitical event, natural disaster, or financial stress — creates a hard production stop approximately 5 weeks after event onset, with no credible alternative sourcing available inside the 18-week lead time window. The supply chain stress model quantifies revenue at risk at $9.1B at the 95th percentile VaR of $2,634M. Recovery is estimated at 5 months. The model further indicates that dual-sourcing remediation could recover $382M in savings and inventory buffering a further $305M, establishing a clear financial case for accelerated action.

Geographic concentration compounds these concerns: 53.5% of spend is Taiwan-domiciled (TSMC and Quanta both), meaning a single-geography shock removes the two most critical suppliers simultaneously. China at 21.8% adds Foxconn exposure.

Yangtze Memory [SUP008], a multi-source supplier, carries a financial health score of 55 — the lowest in the portfolio — and contributes to the two active distress flags (amber threshold: 1, breach threshold: 3). While not yet at breach on distress count, the trajectory of Yangtze Memory warrants proactive monitoring given its proximity to the breach threshold.

Trend: Deteriorating. Two KRIs in breach, no evidence of active dual-sourcing remediation programme in the data provided, and the demand shock probability sits at 24% with a 15% impact if triggered.

---

O-02 CYBER AND IT — ESCALATION TRIGGERED

Four KRIs are in breach; three are amber. The breach profile is wide and structurally concerning.

Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) is 11 days, against a breach threshold of 10 days. With 4 security incidents recorded month-to-date, threats are not hypothetical — they are active. An 11-day detection window means an adversary who gains access today will, on average, operate undetected for 11 days. Mean Time to Respond is 18 days, meaning the full detect-to-contain cycle spans nearly three weeks on a per-incident basis.

Patch compliance stands at 71%, against a breach threshold of 80%. This means approximately 29% of systems are running unpatched. Combined with 8 critical vulnerabilities open beyond 30 days (amber threshold 5, breach threshold 10 — this sits in amber territory at 8 but is approaching breach), the attack surface is materially exposed.

IT RTO is 6 hours — exactly at the breach threshold of 6 hours. There is no buffer. A major incident today would push recovery beyond the acceptable window.

The most structurally dangerous finding is supplier cyber resilience assessment at 0%. Not a single supplier has been assessed for cyber resilience, yet 4 active security incidents have occurred this month. This meets the escalation trigger condition directly. With TSMC, Foxconn, and Quanta all deeply integrated into production systems, an undetected compromise of their environments — invisible to the organisation because no assessment framework exists — could manifest as supply disruption, data exfiltration, or operational sabotage with no early warning.

MFA coverage at 87% and privileged access unreviewed for 45 days add to the control gap. 13% of the estate lacks MFA, and unreviewed privileged access for 45 days is a significant lateral movement and insider threat risk.

The O-02 + O-04 interconnection is critical here: IT-Operations attrition at 11.8% and 7 critical roles open beyond 60 days mean the team responsible for patching, incident response, and vulnerability management is understaffed. This directly explains the patch compliance breach and elevated MTTD — they are symptoms of a talent-constrained security function.

BCM implication: An RTO at exactly 6 hours means any unplanned major incident will breach operational recovery commitments with no margin. Revenue impact would materialise immediately for any outage extending beyond this window across order management systems (IT RTO cited as it_rto_oms).

Trend: Deteriorating. Four KRIs in breach, active incidents, 0% supplier assessment, and a talent pipeline issue suppressing control effectiveness.

---

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — MONITOR; NO SAFETY INCIDENTS

No confirmed safety incidents year-to-date. Zero regulatory product notices. Field failure rate of 0.019% is well within amber threshold of 0.05%. These are genuine positives and the zero-tolerance escalation trigger for safety incidents is not triggered.

However, three indicators warrant attention:

Recall readiness score of 72 is in amber territory, 8 points below the 80 amber threshold and only 2 points above the 70 breach threshold. The last recall simulation was conducted 8 months ago. The combination of a readiness score trending toward breach and an 8-month gap since testing means the organisation's ability to execute a recall — should one be required — is structurally untested and weakening. This is a latent risk: benign today, but the consequence of being unprepared when a quality event occurs is disproportionate in regulatory, reputational, and financial terms.

Supplier quality rejection rate is 0.68%, in amber (threshold: 0.5% amber, 1.0% breach). The specific rejection rates by supplier are visible in O-01 data: Quanta at 1.2% is the highest among single-source suppliers. Quanta's rejection rate is above the 1.0% breach threshold for supplier quality rejection individually. This is notable because Quanta is already flagged for financial distress and minimum inventory cover. A financially stressed supplier with rising rejection rates and thin inventory is a compounding signal, not three independent ones.

Warranty claims of 1,842 month-to-date are provided as a data point; no threshold is specified in the data for this metric, so no status determination is made beyond noting volume.

First pass yield of 97.2% and customer complaint rate of 0.12% indicate that internal production quality is holding. The quality risk is concentrated in the inbound supplier tier, particularly Quanta, rather than in own-manufacturing processes.

EBITDA implication: Warranty claims at 1,842 MTD represent a cost line. If Quanta's rejection rate or financial health deteriorates further, the proportion of defective ODM-Laptop units entering the fulfilment chain could increase, directly expanding warranty cost exposure.

Trend: Flat on safety; deteriorating on recall readiness (score approaching breach, simulation overdue) and supplier rejection (Quanta specifically above individual breach threshold).

---

O-04 TALENT — ESCALATION TRIGGERED

Three flight-risk employees are flagged simultaneously within R&D. This meets the escalation trigger of three or more flight-risk employees in the same critical function. This is escalated immediately.

Engineering attrition is 14.2% annualised — in amber (threshold: 12% amber, 18% breach). IT-Operations attrition is 11.8% — at amber boundary. Both functions are under sustained outflow pressure.

Critical open roles beyond 60 days stands at 7 across the organisation, breaching the threshold of 6. Average time to fill in Engineering is 68 days, meaning even when positions are identified and resourced, the fill cycle extends beyond the 60-day threshold by default. The organisation is structurally unable to close its own vacancy metric at current fill velocity.

Compensation gap versus market in Engineering is 8.2%. This is the clearest causal driver of attrition and flight risk. The counter-offer acceptance rate of 42% in Engineering confirms the dynamic: when departing engineers are counter-offered, fewer than half accept. Either the counter-offers are insufficient, or the departure motivation is non-financial (career, culture, management). Either interpretation is concerning.

SVP succession coverage at 72% is in amber (threshold: 80% amber, 70% breach) — one point above breach. If a single SVP-level departure occurs in a currently uncovered role, this metric breaches. With flight risk concentrated in R&D and an 8.2% compensation gap in the most senior technical functions, the proximity of succession coverage to breach is not theoretical.

Employee engagement at 67% and internal promotion rate at 18% are provided as context metrics. Low engagement and limited internal mobility are consistent with the attrition and flight risk profile and suggest the compensation gap is part of a broader retention challenge, not solely a pay issue.

BCM and revenue implication: R&D flight risk directly threatens the product development pipeline. Loss of 3 key R&D personnel in the same function creates knowledge concentration risk — particularly for capability that is not easily recruited externally in under 68 days. This also intersects with O-01: qualification of alternative suppliers for TSMC and Quanta requires R&D and engineering involvement. Talent loss in these functions will slow dual-sourcing remediation, prolonging the BCM vulnerability quantified in the supply chain stress model.

EBITDA implication: 8.2% compensation gap in Engineering. Closing the gap to market requires incremental compensation spend across the Engineering population. Failure to close it perpetuates attrition and associated recruitment, onboarding, and productivity-loss costs. The counter-offer acceptance rate of 42% suggests the organisation is already incurring counter-offer costs with limited retention return.

Trend: Deteriorating. Two escalation conditions present (flight-risk concentration in R&D; open roles in breach), compensation gap widening pressure, and succession coverage approaching breach.

appetite_thresholds.domain_statement: Low appetite for supply chain disruption and product quality failures. Zero tolerance for major safety incidents. Low tolerance for cyber incidents: MTTD must stay below 5 days; patch compliance must exceed 95%.

# Strategic Risk Assessment — Current Cycle

---

## 1. DOMAIN STATUS
Geopolitical exposure has breached threshold and dominates the risk picture, while AI disruption is amber and M&A integration is holding within acceptable bounds.

---

## 2. S-01 FINDING — GEOPOLITICAL (STATUS: BREACH)
Five geopolitical signals are active this cycle, of which four are rated high severity. The high-severity signals — **"US expands Entity List by 14 technology companies,"** **"Taiwan Strait military exercises increase in frequency,"** and **"Dell launches AI-native laptop line with on-device LLM"** alongside the broader tension pattern — indicate simultaneous supply chain, trade compliance, and competitive pressure vectors. The EU and India signals ("EU announces reciprocal tariff review on Chinese electronics" and "India announces PLI scheme expansion for domestic PC manufacturing") add secondary medium-severity exposure across two additional jurisdictions.

---

## 3. S-02 FINDING — AI DISRUPTION (STATUS: AMBER)
Three competitive signals are active in this domain. Two are directly material: **"Dell launches AI-native laptop line with on-device LLM"** and **"HP announces partnership with Google for Gemini-embedded Chromebook Pro"** represent concrete market moves by primary competitors embedding AI at the device level. A third signal, **"India announces PLI scheme expansion for domestic PC manufacturing,"** adds indirect competitive pressure by potentially accelerating lower-cost AI-capable hardware into global channels.

---

## 4. S-03 FINDING — M&A INTEGRATION (STATUS: OK)
Three deals are active across varying stages: **CloudScale Systems** (ACQ001) is in integration at month 18 with a deal value of USD 1,200M, **NeuralEdge AI** (ACQ002) is in due diligence at USD 450M, and **DataVault Security** (ACQ003) is in negotiation at USD 280M. Synergy delivery is running at **84.0%** against target across the portfolio. While status is OK, CloudScale's 18-month integration tenure and the 16-point gap to full synergy target warrant monitoring.

---

## 5. TOP ACTIONS

| Risk | Action |
|---|---|
| **S-01 Geopolitical** | Immediately audit supplier and technology-partner exposure against the 14 newly listed Entity List companies and assess Taiwan Strait scenario impact on supply continuity. |
| **S-02 AI Disruption** | Accelerate evaluation of the **NeuralEdge AI** acquisition (ACQ002) given direct competitive moves by Dell and HP in on-device AI, and assess whether current product roadmap can respond within the same window. |
| **S-03 M&A Integration** | Conduct a targeted synergy recovery review for **CloudScale Systems** (ACQ001) to close the gap to the 100% synergy target before integration scope drifts further at month 18. |

---

## 6. ESCALATION
**YES** — S-01 Geopolitical KRI is in BREACH status. Escalation to executive leadership and the Risk Committee is required this cycle.

F-01.agent_findings:
## F-01: FX & Commodity Exposure

### FX Positions (Primary KRI Scope per CF-04 2026-06-07)
Total unhedged FX exposure stands at USD 4,080M against an amber threshold of USD 4,000M and a breach threshold of USD 5,000M — status: AMBER. The portfolio average hedge ratio of 46.3% is above the 45% breach floor but below the 60% amber threshold — status: AMBER.

Breakdown by position:
- FX001 USD_CNY (Revenue, JPMorgan): Gross USD 3,200M, hedged USD 1,400M (43.8%), unhedged USD 1,800M. Unrealised P&L: -USD 8.4M. Maturity: 6 months. This is the single largest FX exposure and the most under-hedged major position in ratio terms, with hedge coverage materially below the 60% amber threshold.
- FX002 USD_EUR (Revenue, Deutsche Bank): Gross USD 2,800M, hedged USD 1,200M (42.9%), unhedged USD 1,600M. Unrealised P&L: +USD 7.2M. Maturity: 12 months. Hedge ratio also below amber threshold.
- FX003 USD_JPY (Cost, HSBC): Gross USD 980M, hedged USD 600M (61.2%), unhedged USD 380M. Unrealised P&L: -USD 15.6M. Maturity: 3 months. This is the only FX position with a hedge ratio above the 60% amber threshold, though the 3-month maturity creates near-term roll risk given the unrealised loss position.
- FX004 USD_KRW (Cost, Citibank): Gross USD 620M, hedged USD 320M (51.6%), unhedged USD 300M. Unrealised P&L: -USD 9.8M. Maturity: 6 months.

FX-only unrealised P&L (KRI scope): -USD 47.0M. This figure flows through operating income into EBITDA.

### Commodity Positions (Supplementary Context — Not in KRI Scope)
- COM001 DRAM (Goldman Sachs): Gross USD 890M, hedged USD 400M (44.9%), unhedged USD 490M. Unrealised P&L: -USD 12.3M. Maturity: 3 months.
- COM002 NAND (JPMorgan): Gross USD 650M, hedged USD 280M (43.1%), unhedged USD 370M. Unrealised P&L: -USD 8.1M. Maturity: 3 months.
Commodity unrealised P&L: -USD 20.4M. This flows through COGS into gross profit and then EBITDA.

Total portfolio unrealised P&L (FX -47.0M + commodity -20.4M): -USD 67.4M. If fully realised, this compresses EBITDA headroom from USD 152M to approximately USD 85M before COV001 breach — a critical cross-domain stress scenario.

Trend assessment for F-01: DETERIORATING. Unhedged exposure has crossed the amber threshold. All revenue FX positions (USD_CNY, USD_EUR) carry hedge ratios below 45% amber threshold. Commodity maturities of 3 months on both DRAM and NAND positions create near-term roll and mark-to-market risk. The aggregate unrealised loss of -USD 67.4M against an EBITDA headroom of only USD 152M is a material concentration of mark-to-market risk relative to covenant headroom.

---

## F-02: Revenue Concentration & Credit Quality

### Top Customer Concentration
Top customer revenue concentration is 22.1%, attributable to the Enterprise Tier1 segment — status: AMBER (amber threshold: >20%, breach threshold: >25%). This single concentration point represents the dominant credit and revenue risk in the AR portfolio. A payment failure or impairment event from this counterparty would simultaneously increase bad_debt_provision (compounding the COV006 breach) and reduce revenue recognition.

### Bad Debt Provision
Bad debt provision stands at 1.44% of AR — status: BREACH (amber: >0.40%, breach: >0.55%, COV006 covenant threshold: 0.80%). The provision has breached both the internal risk appetite threshold and the Trade-Finance facility covenant. Segment breakdown shows SMB Tier3 (USD 32.1M provision) and Enterprise Tier2 (USD 28.6M provision) as the largest contributors to absolute provision balances, followed by Consumer Tier4 (USD 18.4M). Overdue_90d balances total USD 60.0M across all segments: Enterprise Tier2 USD 18M, SMB Tier3 USD 22M, Consumer Tier4 USD 12M, Enterprise Tier1 USD 8M, Government Tier1 USD 0M.

Trend assessment for F-02: DETERIORATING on bad_debt_provision (confirmed breach, worsening from prior test). Top customer concentration flat at 22.1% — no directional signal available from current data alone.

---

## F-03: Liquidity & Refinancing — Covenant Detail

### COV001 Net Debt/EBITDA (RCF-USD2B)
Current: 2.8x vs threshold 3.0x — status: AMBER. Headroom: 0.2x in ratio units, equivalent to USD 152M of EBITDA headroom (derived: Net Debt = 2.8 × USD 2.28B = USD 6.384B; EBITDA floor = USD 6.384B / 3.0 = USD 2.128B; headroom = USD 2.28B − USD 2.128B = USD 0.152B). This covenant deteriorated from 2.6x (Mar-26 test) to 2.8x (Jun-26 test). Stress scenarios:
- Full COV006 cure write-off (USD 94.3M): Residual EBITDA headroom ≈ USD 58M before COV001 breach.
- Partial cure write-off (USD 50M): Residual EBITDA headroom ≈ USD 102M before COV001 breach.
- Full portfolio mark-to-market realisation (USD 67.4M): Residual EBITDA headroom ≈ USD 85M.
- Combined full cure + portfolio realisation: Would exceed USD 152M headroom and trigger COV001 breach.
Next test: 2026-06-30.

### COV002 Interest Coverage (Term-Loan-A)
Current: 4.2x vs threshold 3.5x — status: OK. Headroom: 0.7x. Declined from 4.8x (Mar-26). Direction is compressing but adequate headroom remains. Next test: 2026-06-30.

### COV003 Liquidity Headroom (RCF-USD2B)
Current: USD 1.1B vs threshold USD 0.8B — status: OK. Headroom: USD 0.3B. Declined from USD 1.2B (Mar-26). USD 200M above the USD 0.9B escalation trigger. Directional compression warrants monitoring; if trend continues at USD 100M per quarter, the escalation trigger would be reached within one quarter. Next test: 2026-06-30.

### COV004 Debt Maturity Runway (Bond-2027)
Current: 8 months vs threshold 12 months — status: AMBER. Headroom: 0 months (covenant already breached in threshold terms — current value is below covenant threshold, but this is categorised amber in source data). Declined from 11 months (Mar-26). Above the 6-month escalation trigger. Refinancing or extension of Bond-2027 must be initiated. Next test: 2026-12-01. If no action is taken and runway reaches 6 months, immediate escalation will be required.

### COV005 EBITDA (All-Facilities)
Current: USD 2.28B vs threshold USD 1.80B — status: OK. Headroom: USD 0.48B. Declined from USD 2.51B (Mar-26), a USD 230M quarter-on-quarter reduction. See stress analysis under COV001 above.

### COV006 Bad Debt Provision (Trade-Finance) — CONFIRMED ACTIVE BREACH
Current: 1.44% vs threshold 0.80% — status: CONFIRMED ACTIVE BREACH. Excess: 0.64 percentage points. This represents a direct breach of the Trade-Finance facility covenant. The breach was present at the Mar-26 test (1.10% vs 0.80%) and has deepened at the Jun-26 test (1.44%). This is not a probabilistic forward risk — it is a confirmed contractual breach requiring immediate action.

#### EXECUTIVE RECOMMENDATION — COV006 CURE/WAIVER (MANDATORY)
(a) BOARD DIRECTIVE REQUIRED: Lender notification under the Trade-Finance facility is a board-authorised act under credit facility terms. No lender contact may occur without prior written board authorisation.
(b) Written Board Authorisation: The Board must pass a formal resolution authorising the Group Treasurer and CFO to notify lenders of the COV006 breach and to negotiate a waiver or cure plan. This resolution must be documented before any lender communication.
(c) Hard Deadline: Board resolution and lender notification must be completed by 2026-06-12.
(d) Named Joint Owners: Group Treasurer and CFO are joint owners of the notification and cure process. CFO co-signature is required on all lender communications per facility terms.
(e) Cross-Default Risk: Failure to notify lenders of the COV006 breach before the 2026-06-30 test date creates cross-default risk across interconnected facilities. All facilities referencing cross-default clauses must be reviewed for exposure.

Cure scenarios to be presented to lenders:
- Full write-off scenario: USD 94.3M flows through P&L, reducing EBITDA headroom to USD ~58M before COV001 breach. This scenario creates secondary COV001 stress and must be modelled against the Jun-30 test.
- Partial cure (USD 50M write-off): EBITDA headroom reduces to USD ~102M — more manageable but does not fully resolve the breach.
- Waiver request: If cure cannot be completed by 2026-06-12, a temporary waiver from Trade-Finance lenders is the alternative path. Waiver terms and duration must be negotiated and documented.

Trend assessment for F-03: DETERIORATING across all covenants. COV001 ratio compressed 0.2x in one quarter; EBITDA declined USD 230M; liquidity headroom declined USD 100M; debt maturity runway shortened 3 months. COV006 is in confirmed active breach with deepening excess.

---

## F-04: Financial Audit & Reporting Controls

AUD003 Internal-SOX Financial Controls audit is completed. Open high findings: 1. Critical findings: 0. Material weaknesses: 0 — status: OK (amber threshold: ≥2 high findings, breach threshold: ≥5 high findings). The single open high finding is below the amber threshold. No escalation is required under F-04 standing criteria. The absence of any material weakness is noted; however, the single high finding should be tracked to resolution given the heightened financial risk environment signalled by F-02 and F-03 this period.

Trend assessment for F-04: FLAT. Single finding at completion, no material weakness, no directional change signalled by current data.

board_summary:
RISK POSTURE

The organisation records 17 KRI breaches and 18 amber warnings across all four domains simultaneously, triggering the systemic risk flag for concurrent multi-domain breach.

STRATEGIC: Geopolitical exposure has breached threshold, with five signals detected under S-01, prompting formal escalation. Two further amber warnings cover AI disruption and M&A integration; the Chief Executive Officer is accountable.

OPERATIONAL: Ten breaches and seven ambers make this the most severely stressed domain this cycle. Supply chain concentration, cyber resilience gaps, attrition, and quality risks interact across multiple compound scenarios; accountability is shared by the COO, CISO, and CHRO by sub-domain. Single-source spend stands at 62.6% against a 50% breach threshold. Supplier cyber resilience assessment is at 0%, leaving TSMC, Foxconn, and Quanta cyber compromises undetectable. Quanta carries the highest rejection rate at 1.2%, a financial health score of 61, and only 3.9 weeks inventory — simultaneously threatening quality and buffer. IT-Operations attrition of 11.8% leaves seven critical roles open beyond 60 days, directly impairing remediation of eight open critical vulnerabilities, an 11-day MTTD breach, and 71% patch compliance. Three R&D flight-risk employees concentrate innovation and supplier-qualification capability; their loss could obstruct dual-sourcing remediation estimated to save USD 382M.

FINANCIAL: Four breaches and seven ambers are recorded; the Chief Financial Officer is accountable. Combined unrealised portfolio losses of USD 67.4M — FX USD 47.0M across JPMorgan, HSBC, and Citibank, and commodity USD 20.4M across Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan — would compress COV001 Net Debt/EBITDA headroom from USD 152M to approximately USD 85M if realised. A bad debt breach of 1.44% against a 0.80% threshold, with a USD 94.3M full-cure write-off, further reduces that headroom to approximately USD 58M. Total unhedged FX exposure is USD 4,080M, amber against a USD 5,000M breach threshold, with a portfolio hedge ratio of 46.3%. USD/CNY is the largest single position at USD 3,200M gross, only 43.8% hedged, carrying explicit geopolitical sensitivity cross-referenced to S-01. Three active acquisitions — CloudScale Systems, NeuralEdge AI, and DataVault Security — may shift customer revenue concentration in amber territory; the Enterprise Tier-1 segment alone represents 22.1% of revenue, constituting the highest single-point credit concentration risk in the portfolio.

COMPLIANCE: Two breaches and two amber warnings are recorded, with the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel jointly accountable. Two export licences expire within 30 days — ECC-2024-0047 covering TSMC components and ECC-2024-0061 covering DRAM modules — both requiring CCO sign-off. The CCO concurrently owns the FCPA and UK Bribery Act ABAC programme, where AUD001 is complete with two high findings and AUD002 Tier-2 is in progress to 2026-06-30, as well as the in-progress export control audit AUD005, creating capacity concentration risk across C-01 and C-03. Three denied-party matches remain pending resolution, up from two in the prior period; a potential but unconfirmed overlap with ABAC-registered suppliers requires immediate cross-check between the Trade Compliance System and the ABAC supplier registry.

KEY RISK DRIVERS

The most consequential combination of findings this period is the simultaneous breach of the Trade-Finance covenant, active cross-default risk, and a near-even probability of a further covenant breach — occurring at the same time as five geopolitical signals are active against a three-signal threshold, inventory cover has fallen below the four-week floor to 3.9 weeks, and the cyber detection window has widened to 11 days. What makes this period materially different from a multi-domain amber picture is that these are not independent risks trending in the wrong direction: they are interconnected failure pathways that can trigger each other within a single quarter. The confirmed breach of COV006 on bad debt provision — currently at 1.44% against a 0.80% covenant threshold — requires lender notification before the 30 June test date; failure to act creates cross-default exposure across interconnected facilities. The remaining headroom of USD 152 million against the Net Debt to EBITDA covenant is being simultaneously compressed by a potential USD 94.3 million bad debt write-off and USD 67.4 million in combined unrealised FX and commodity losses — both of which, if realised together, would eliminate headroom entirely and trigger a second covenant breach. Against this financial fragility, the supply chain position adds a hard operational constraint: with 62.6% of USD 12.6 billion in spend single-sourced, geographic concentration at 75.3% — fractionally above the 75% threshold — and zero percent of suppliers assessed for cyber resilience, the organisation cannot detect a compromise of TSMC, Foxconn, or Quanta, and has less than one week of inventory buffer below its own required floor. The board's primary judgement this period is whether the covenant position can be stabilised before the 30 June test date while simultaneously executing emergency supply chain and cyber remediation — because a disruption in any one of these three areas will accelerate deterioration in the others.

CROSS-DOMAIN CONNECTIONS

The most structurally dangerous interaction this period runs from geopolitics through currency exposure into the covenant structure. Three of the five active geopolitical signals are rated high severity, and the largest single unhedged currency exposure — USD 1,800 million on the USD/CNY revenue pair — sits directly in the geopolitical line of fire, with hedge coverage at only 43.8% against a 60% amber target. If geopolitical escalation drives an adverse currency move on that position, unrealised losses would increase and flow through EBITDA, further compressing the USD 152 million of covenant headroom that is already being eroded by the bad debt position. A second, less visible interaction connects the talent and cyber domains in a way that creates direct revenue risk rather than simply an IT control gap. Seven critical roles have been open beyond 60 days and IT-Operations attrition is running at 11.8% annualised; this staffing deficit is the structural reason that patch compliance stands at 71%, mean time to detect has reached 11 days, and eight critical vulnerabilities remain open. An understaffed security function responding to four active incidents this month, with an IT recovery time already at its six-hour limit, creates a scenario in which a successful intrusion during this period would exceed recovery commitments and result in direct revenue loss — this is not an IT audit finding, it is a trading continuity exposure. A third interaction connects the talent domain to supply chain remediation: the three R&D flight-risk employees flagged by the operational agent are the same population needed to qualify alternative suppliers for TSMC and Quanta. Loss of those individuals would obstruct the dual-sourcing programme that domain agents estimate could protect up to USD 382 million in exposure, meaning talent attrition directly prolongs the supply chain breach. Finally, the CCO's concurrent ownership of the export controls audit, the ABAC programme, and two export licences expiring within 30 days creates a single-point execution risk in the compliance domain: if CCO capacity is consumed by the ABAC Tier-2 audit due on 30 June, the licence renewals and three pending denied-party matches may not receive timely attention, and an expired licence covering TSMC components would compound the supply chain exposure already in breach.

QUARTER-ON-QUARTER MOVEMENT

Aggregate residual risk has risen materially from FY26Q1 to FY26Q2, with 15 of 20 tracked KRIs moving in an adverse direction and four KRIs crossing into breach status for the first time this period: mean time to detect (moving from amber to breach at 11 days against a 10-day threshold), critical open roles beyond 60 days (moving from amber to breach at 7 against a threshold of 6), inventory cover (moving from amber to breach at 3.9 weeks against a 4-week floor), and geopolitical signal count (moving from amber to breach at 5 signals against a threshold of 3). The dominant cause of this movement is inherent risk growth — the underlying threat environment has worsened, geopolitical signals have more than doubled, inventory buffers have thinned, and the bad debt provision has continued to deepen from 1.24% to 1.44%, remaining in breach throughout. Control weakening is a secondary but reinforcing factor: patch compliance has moved further into breach territory from 78% to 71%, the hedge ratio has moved further below target from 50.3% to 46.3%, and recall readiness has deteriorated from 82% to 72%, crossing into amber. Three KRIs have improved: AI audit coverage has moved from 74% to 78%, third-party ABAC coverage from 85% to 89%, and M&A synergy delivery from 65% to 84% — all genuine progress, though all three remain in breach or at amber. The single KRI whose reversal would most reduce residual risk this period is mean time to detect: closing the detection window is the precondition for all other cyber remediation to have effect, and it is the lever that, if pulled, most directly reduces the probability of a revenue-loss incident during the current period of elevated geopolitical and operational stress.

RISK COMMITTEE RECOMMENDED ACTIONS:
  • Direct the CFO to present a formal covenant cure plan at this meeting or within 48 hours, addressing the existing covenant breach, the active cross-default risk, and the near-even probability of a further breach — and to confirm whether lender notification obligations have been triggered. The Committee must be advised that combined downside pressure from a USD 94.3 million bad debt write-off and USD 67.4 million in unrealised hedging losses could eliminate the remaining USD 152 million of covenant headroom entirely.
  • Direct the COO and VP Supply Chain to submit an emergency dual-sourcing and inventory recovery plan within 14 days, covering the three single-source suppliers, the 3.9-week inventory shortfall against the 4-week threshold, and the 75.3% geographic concentration breach. The Committee should note that domain agents estimate successful dual-sourcing remediation could protect up to USD 382 million in exposure, and that the plan must address the zero percent supplier cyber resilience assessment as a precondition to any continuity assurance.
  • Require the CISO to present a time-bound remediation schedule for all five breached cyber KRIs — mean time to detect at 11 days, patch compliance at 71%, multi-factor authentication coverage at 87%, privileged access reviews at 45 days overdue, and IT recovery time at its 6-hour limit — with specific milestones for each. The Committee must also direct the CISO and CHRO jointly to address the 7 critical roles open beyond 60 days and 11.8% annualised IT-Operations attrition, which domain agents have identified as a structural impediment to cyber remediation.
  • Direct the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel to confirm to the Committee, within 7 days, that the two export licences expiring within 30 days have been renewed or that operations dependent on them have been suspended, and to provide a resolution timeline for the three pending denied-party matches. The Committee should note that concurrent ownership of the export controls and ABAC programmes creates a capacity risk identified by domain agents, and should require the CCO to confirm adequate resourcing across both obligations given the active audit of Tier-2 third parties.
  • Require the CHRO to provide the Committee with a retention and succession status report covering the 7 critical open roles breaching the 60-day threshold and the three R&D flight-risk employees identified by domain agents as critical to alternative supplier qualification. The Committee should direct the CHRO and COO to confirm that the loss of any of these individuals would not further delay dual-sourcing remediation, given the USD 382 million exposure estimate attached to that programme.

cov001_board_narrative.text: IMPORTANT — COV001 AMBER RATIO VS FORWARD BREACH PROBABILITY: The Net Debt to EBITDA ratio currently reads 2.8× (amber — below the 3.0× breach ceiling). This ratio reflects the position as at the Q2 reporting date and is correct. However, the EBITDA Stress Monte Carlo separately estimates a 69.4% probability that this ratio will exceed 3.0× by the 2026-06-30 test date — a breach-status forward indicator. These two signals are not contradictory: the ratio measures where the organisation is today; the Monte Carlo measures where it is likely to be in 23 days under plausible EBITDA scenarios. Both signals must be read together. The amber ratio does NOT indicate the covenant position is under control — the forward probability of 69.4% means there is a greater-than-two-thirds chance of a COV001 breach at the next test date. The board should treat this as a breach-trajectory situation requiring the same urgency as an active breach. A deterministic scenario in which the COV006 provision charge of approximately USD 365M is recognised in full would reduce EBITDA to approximately USD 1,915M — below the COV001 implied floor of USD 2,128M — giving P(breach) = 100% at test date.
